issues.body:
Observed on 2026-05-20 in E:\swarmauri_github\bucketwarden during a three-worker SSOT MCP T3 campaign.

Main-thread and worker observations:
- Three parallel T3 workers were launched against the downstream repo and instructed to loop on claim -> context -> implement/test/evidence -> complete.
- No worker obtained a lease.
- Open blocked transitions reported widespread missing_target_tier_claim_wiring across many features, especially T0 -> T1.
- MCP-side scaffold_target_claim_wiring could create missing claim/test/evidence rows for individual blocked features.
- Some blocked transitions could also be resolved through repair_blocked_transition, but queue progress remained inconsistent.
- After targeted repair, repeated claim_next_maturation_slice attempts often hung and timed out instead of returning a lease, returning an exhausted/no-work result, or returning a stable blocker set.
- At least one repair path failed with a Windows filesystem error: WinError 5 while renaming a temporary registry file onto .ssot/registry.json.
- One blocked_id surfaced by get_blocked_transitions could not later be repaired because repair_blocked_transition reported unknown open blocked transition.
- Workers therefore could not honestly proceed to leased runtime work, focused verification, or T3 evidence closure.

Downstream impact:
- Honest T3 worker campaigns can burn time on MCP-side repair/scaffolding without ever receiving lease ownership for the next actionable slice.
- Certification-closure work in downstream repos is blocked even when queue inspection still suggests claimable work exists.
- Operators cannot tell whether the correct next action is more repair, a claim retry, lease recovery, or MCP service restart.

Expected closure:
- After targeted blocker repair, worker claim-next should deterministically do one of the following: issue a lease, return a stable scoped blocker set, or report that no claimable slice remains.
- blocked_id values returned by blocker/status surfaces should remain repairable for the advertised open transition.
- Registry write paths used during repair/claim should tolerate or clearly surface Windows temp-file rename failures instead of leaving workers in a retry loop with no lease.
- The worker loop should not require downstream operators to guess whether scaffold-only changes without lease ownership are the intended recovery path.

Concrete downstream evidence from this run:
- Worker codex-t3-c6f347 repaired at least one blocker for feat:bucketwarden.cli.cmd.help, then claim retries timed out.
- Worker codex-t3-932fb4a1 resolved a blocker for feat:bucketwarden.cli.cmd.diagnostics, still received no lease, and hit WinError 5 during registry temp-file rename.
- Worker codex-t3-e0cb8291 repaired/scaffolded feat:bucketwarden.audit-evidence-log and resolved blk:4221a429c1f145f2ab2e373a5858a8da, but subsequent claim attempts still timed out.
- Main thread confirmed broad open blocker queues and that downstream repo changes were limited to scaffold/evidence/test surfaces rather than leased runtime completion.
issues.description: A downstream three-worker T3 maturation campaign in bucketwarden remained unable to obtain leases even after MCP-side blocker repair and target-tier scaffolding.
issues.title: Downstream T3 worker campaigns can stall after blocker repair without issuing a lease

issues.body:
Observed on 2026-05-20 while trying to follow up a downstream SSOT MCP campaign failure by adding new governed feature and test rows in E:\swarmauri_github\ssot-registry.

What happened:
- A new downstream issue was created successfully for the T3 worker campaign lease/repair failure mode.
- The next step was to create new SSOT feature and test rows covering the newly observed failure classes.
- feature create failed because the new in-bound feature had no linked claims and tests yet.
- test create failed because the new test had no linked claims and evidence yet.
- Retrying feature create with forward references to the intended claim_ids and test_ids also failed because those referenced rows did not exist yet.
- The CLI surface available here does not expose a single atomic authoring command that can create a valid feature, claim, test, and evidence graph in one transaction.

Downstream impact:
- Operators can create issues that describe new failure modes, but they cannot cleanly extend governed feature/test coverage for those failures through the same CLI surface without dropping to non-atomic or out-of-band mutation.
- This blocks honest follow-up work when new runtime or control-plane problems are discovered and need immediate SSOT feature/test rows.
- The current surface effectively requires the proof graph to already exist before any one member of the graph can be created, which prevents incremental governed authoring.

Expected closure:
- Provide an atomic CLI authoring path for new governed proof graphs, or
- provide a scaffold flow that creates a minimally valid linked feature-claim-test-evidence graph in one operation, or
- relax validation for explicitly planned draft/planned entities in a way that still preserves honesty while allowing incremental authoring.

Minimum acceptance expectations:
- A user should be able to create a new current-horizon in-bound feature plus its initial linked claim/test/evidence scaffolding through supported CLI flows alone.
- The CLI should clearly document which multi-entity creation path is intended when immediate validation requires a complete graph.
- The failure should not force direct registry edits or hidden implementation knowledge.

Concrete command outcomes from this run:
- feature create for new ssot-mcp follow-up features failed with registry validation errors requiring linked claims and tests.
- test create for new planned pytest rows failed with registry validation errors requiring linked claims and evidence rows.
- feature create with forward-linked nonexistent claim_ids/test_ids also failed, so the surface currently deadlocks incremental graph authoring.
issues.description: Creating a new governed feature, test, or claim graph through the SSOT CLI can fail because validation requires the full linked proof graph immediately, while the CLI does not expose a single atomic create flow for that graph.
issues.title: SSOT CLI cannot incrementally author new in-bound proof graphs

issues.body:
Observed on 2026-05-21 while running a multi-worker SSOT MCP closure attempt against `downstream-user`.

What happened:
- Multiple boundary-focused workers (`pdfua1`, `pdfua2`, `tagged-pdf`, `wtpdf`, `rails.migration-targets.*`) reported `campaign_complete` and received no lease, even though the downstream slice rows were still not honestly closed.
- Local runtime and focused tests were real and passing for several slices, but downstream SSOT rows remained stale: many slice features were still marked `implementation_status: absent`, and many boundary tests were still `planned` with missing claim/evidence wiring.
- A downstream issue body was prepared for empirical PDF target-probe blockers, but `ssot issue create` in the downstream repo failed because post-create validation ran against unrelated whole-registry drift.
- `ssot validate .` in the downstream repo reduced to the expected schema blocker (`schema_version 0.6.0` vs supported `0.7.0`), but `ssot upgrade . --sync-docs --write-report` still failed because the upgrade path enforced broad structural and proof requirements across the entire legacy registry before completing migration.
- The downstream work therefore stalled in a state where operators had concrete blocker reports and real runtime/test evidence, but could not record the issue in the governed registry and could not advance the registry schema to the supported version.

Downstream impact:
- Honest worker campaigns can report `complete` without leasing work even when a boundary is not truly closed.
- Downstream operators cannot reliably distinguish `no actionable work remains` from `closure is blocked by stale registry state`.
- A downstream repo can have enough truth to justify issue filing and migration planning, yet issue creation fails because the command validates unrelated global drift after attempting the mutation.
- Legacy-schema downstream registries can become effectively un-upgradable in practice because the upgrade path requires broad proof/consistency closure before the schema bump and doc sync can finish.

Concrete downstream evidence from the run:
- `pdfua1`, `pdfua2`, `tagged-pdf`, `wtpdf`, and `rails.migration-targets.*` workers all observed the same no-lease `campaign_complete` pattern.
- Focused downstream tests still passed, including:
  - focused checks for one downstream validation slice
  - focused checks for a second downstream validation slice
  - focused checks for the downstream tagging slice
  - focused checks for the downstream transport slice
  - focused checks for the downstream migration slice
- A prepared downstream issue body exists in the downstream workspace, but could not be filed through SSOT because create-time validation failed on unrelated registry drift.
- Manual repair reduced some drift, but downstream `upgrade --sync-docs` still failed on deeper structural/proof constraints rather than executing a tolerant schema migration for a legacy registry.

Expected closure:
- Worker claim and campaign status should not report `complete` for scoped downstream slices when the slice rows are still materially stale or blocked from honest closure.
- `issue create` should support a governed path that can persist an issue in the presence of unrelated baseline registry drift, at least when the new issue does not worsen validation.
- `upgrade --sync-docs` should provide a legacy-registry migration path that can advance schema and synchronize documents before requiring full modern proof-chain closure across unrelated surfaces.
- Upgrade/reporting surfaces should clearly separate:
  - schema migration blockers
  - whole-registry integrity drift
  - proof/claim/test/evidence closure gaps
  - scoped downstream mutation safety

Minimum acceptance expectations:
- A downstream repo on an older schema can still record a high-signal governed issue about newly observed blockers without first repairing every unrelated registry inconsistency.
- A downstream operator can run upgrade and receive a migrated schema/doc state plus a precise inventory of post-migration closure work, instead of being blocked before migration completes.
- Scoped worker campaigns should expose a stable `blocked by stale registry state` outcome instead of collapsing into misleading `campaign_complete` when no honest lease can be issued.
issues.description: Downstream worker campaigns, issue filing, and schema upgrade can all stall on baseline registry drift before scoped closure work can proceed honestly.
issues.title: Legacy downstream registries can block issue filing and upgrade before honest closure work starts
